feed.title: Combined summary - Giving teeth to expected EC disabling: P2XX(-T)(-ML)

entry.title: Combined summary - Giving teeth to expected EC disabling: P2XX(-T)(-ML)
entry.summary: The ongoing discussions within the Bitcoin development community focus on enhancing security measures in response to potential threats from quantum computing developments. Two primary mechanisms, Tripwire and Miner Lockdown, have been proposed to manage the automatic disabling of Elliptic Curve (EC) operation codes/paths for new output types like P2TRv2. The Tripwire mechanism, as suggested by Tadge Dryja, uses a specific transaction type as a trigger to deactivate EC operations immediately, ensuring that the disabling does not get indefinitely postponed. In contrast, the Miner Lockdown mechanism allows a majority of the mining hashrate to initiate the disabling, offering a faster response option to emerging threats. Boris Nagaev's contributions highlight concerns with these mechanisms, particularly the risk of false-positive activations in the Miner Lockdown feature, where conventional security breaches could mistakenly trigger a cryptographic reaction. He suggests that there should be a capability to reactivate the EC path if it is found that the lockdown was triggered by such a false alarm. He also questions the extent of the disabling, whether it affects all EC-related paths or only the main spending path, which is crucial for ensuring that legitimate operations are not unnecessarily halted. Further discussion points raised include the implementation timing and impacts of the Miner Lockdown mechanism. It is suggested that a lengthy period between signaling and activation phases could allow users more flexibility in adapting to changes. Concerns about miners' willingness to take on the responsibilities associated with this mechanism were also noted, highlighting the need for early identification of any opposition to adjust the proposal accordingly. Moreover, the dialogue touched on potential inefficiencies arising from users needing to revert from new transaction output types back to P2TR due to CRQC-protection goals. This change could lead to significant chain capacity issues. A critical viewpoint emphasized the need to disable certain functionalities within new output types to maintain security objectives, with proposals including the introduction of an OP_CHECKHYBRIDECSQISIGN opcode to address hybrid signature schemes distinctly at the opcode level. Lastly, the discussion explored technical considerations around the tripwire mechanism for disabling EC spending post-quantum transition. The approach focuses on using discrete logarithm signatures as proof, avoiding the need for trustless rewards or on-chain inclusion for historical records. Concerns about premature activations by miners, motivated by transaction fee increases, were highlighted, suggesting that nodes should have the capability to reject such activations. The email concludes with a reflection on the complexities of implementing hybrid cryptographic systems, opting instead for simpler, more isolated paths for future security needs.
